items.title: All the evidence unveiled so far in Musk v. Altman
items.description:

items.content: The Musk v. Altman trial is underway, and that means exhibits, or the evidence to be presented in court, are being revealed piece by piece. So far, email exchanges, photos, and corporate documents are circulating from the earliest days of OpenAI — and from before the AI lab even had a name. Some high-level takeaways: Nvidia CEO Jensen Huang gave OpenAI an in-demand supercomputer, Musk largely drafted OpenAI’s mission and heavily influenced its early structure, OpenAI CEO Sam Altman appeared to want to lean heavily on Y Combinator for early support for OpenAI, OpenAI president Greg Brockman and Ilya Sutskever worried about Musk’s level of control over the company, and Musk highlighted the importance of a nonprofit with a mission of broadly beneficial AI.Musk’s buzzy lawsuit, which began its jury trial on Monday in a federal courtroom in California, names Altman, Brockman, and OpenAI investor Microsoft as defendants. It accuses them of breaching the company’s charitable trust, fraud, and unjust enrichment, but ultimately, Musk’s lawsuit boils down to whether or not OpenAI deviated from its founding mission of ensuring that artificial general intelligence — an often vaguely-defined term that denotes AI systems that equal or surpass human intelligence — benefits all of humanity. It’s the latest in a yearslong string of legal actions against OpenAI and its executives by Musk, who co-founded the AI lab alongside Altman and Brockman and was an early investor. (Musk also owns xAI, an AI lab that directly competes with OpenAI, and is owned by parent company SpaceX.)Former OpenAI employees and people close to both companies have been watching this particular lawsuit with a close eye, since the outcome of a jury trial could have affected how OpenAI runs its business and controls its quickly-advancing technology. Plus, OpenAI and SpaceX are both reportedly racing to go public this year, so they’re more in the public eye than ever.The lawsuit discovery process had already unearthed a lot of eyebrow-raising communications between AI industry executives, from emails between Altman and Sutskever to entries from Brockman’s own diary. Even texts between Meta CEO Mark Zuckerberg and Musk were made public. But that was all before the jury trial started — now, there’s even more set to be revealed.Here’s an exhaustive list of all the exhibits that have been made public so far and the biggest takeaways from each one. Admittedly, not every item is necessarily interesting, so we’ve flagged the most important ones with an asterisk The Verge will keep updating the list as more are added.*Exhibit No. 5A June 2015 email exchange between Altman and Musk. Altman lays out a five-part plan, involving an AI lab with a mission to “create the first general AI and use it for individual empowerment—ie, the distributed version of the future that seems the safest. More generally, safety should be a first-class requirement.”He suggests that they start with seven to 10 people and expand from there, using an extra Y Combinator building located in Mountain View. Governance-wise, Altman names five people to start, proposing himself, Musk, Bill Gates, Pierre Omidyar, and Dustin Moskovitz. “The technology would be owned by the foundation and used ‘for the good of the world’, and in cases where it’s not obvious how that should be applied the 5 of us would decide,” Altman writes. He adds that the researchers working at the lab would have “significant financial upside … uncorrelated to what they build, which should eliminate some of the conflict,” and suggests paying them a “competitive salary” and awarding them equity in Y Combinator. He also says they should get someone to “run the team” but that that person “probably shouldn’t be on the governance board.”Altman goes on to ask Musk whether he’ll be involved in the AI lab in addition to governance, potentially coming by once a month to talk about progress or at least being publicly supportive to help with recruiting. As a model, he names Peter Thiel’s “part-time partner” involvement at Y Combinator.Finally, Altman mentions a “regulation letter,” seeming to imply that the AI lab was going to write a letter calling for AI regulation. He says he’s happy to leave Musk off as a public signatory.Musk replies, “Agree on all.”*Exhibit No. 7In an October 2015 email exchange between Altman and Musk, Altman suggests starting with a $100 million commitment by Musk and asks if he could donate an additional $30 million over the next five years.” He says Bill Gates isn’t yet committed to donating but that he hopes to “have him locked down next week,” adding that he believes Mark Zuckerberg likely won’t come through due to his own AI lab, Facebook AI Research (FAIR). He also suggests that he and Musk start as the first two members of the Safety Board with the potential to add three other members over the following year, calling it the “‘second key’ for releasing anything that could be dangerous.”Musk responds, “Let’s discuss governance. This is critical. I don’t want to fund something that goes in what turns out to be the wrong direction.”*Exhibit No. 12In a November 2015 email exchange between Musk and Altman, the two discuss plans for the forthcoming AI lab. Musk starts off by recounting a “great call with Greg [Brockman]” and saying he’s “super impressed with everyone so far,” calling it a “great team.” He suggests creating the lab as an “independent, pure play 501c3, but with a crystal clear focus on the positive advent of strong AI distributed widely to humanity.” He says the company would “still aim to bring in revenue in excess of costs at some point, but positive net revenue would just flow to cash reserves.”With regards to compensation for employees, Musk suggests a cash salary and certain bonuses. He says that if Altman is amenable, employees could convert cash to stock in Y Combinator, adding that it’s fine if they’d rather convert some or all to SpaceX stock instead. (“I can pretty much do what I want on the SpaceX side, as it is private (thank goodness),” Musk writes.) He also offers “insane amounts of real world sensor data” from Tesla for the AI lab to use, mentioning that the amount of data is “several orders of magnitude greater than any other company.”Musk’s first stab at a name for the AI lab is “Freemind,” as he says it “conveys the sense that we are trying to create digital intelligence that will be freely available to all — the opposite of Deepmind’s one-ring-to-rule-them-all approach.” He also says he’ll dedicate whatever amount of his time is useful, even though that could mean less time allocated to SpaceX and Tesla. “If I really believe that this is potentially the biggest near-term existential threat, then action should follow belief,” he writes. He adds later that, despite seemingly trying to be essentially a silent partner, he has to “bite the bullet on admitting real involvement. This will come as a shocker to many, but so be it. Can’t be lukewarm about this.”Altman suggests the AI lab share a building with Y Combinator and use the incubator’s legal team to help get it started. He also suggests the names “Axon” or something related to famed computer scientist and mathematician Alan Turing.Musk writes, “Something Turing-related that doesn’t sound too ominous might be good. Want to avoid the Turing Test association though, as that sounds too much like we are replacing humans.”*Exhibit No. 14A December 2015 email exchange between Altman and Musk drafts the opening paragraphs of OpenAI’s mission and press release. Musk says the “whole point of this release is to attract top talent.” The two go back and forth on wording, and the final product ends up not straying too much from Musk’s original draft.Musk writes in his draft that “the outcome of this venture is uncertain and the pay is low compared to what others will offer, but we believe the goal and the structure are right.” Altman writes in his draft that “because we don’t have any financial obligations, we can focus on the maximal positive human impact and disseminating AI technology as broadly as possible.”Exhibit No. 16OpenAI’s official articles of incorporation, filed December 8, 2015. The document states that OpenAI “shall be a nonprofit corporation organized exclusively for charitable purposes” and that its purpose is “to ensure that artificial general intelligence benefits all of humanity, including by conducting and/or funding artificial intelligence research. The corporation may also research and/or otherwise support efforts to safely develop and distribute such technology and its associated benefits, including analyzing the societal impacts of the technology and supporting related educational, economic, and safety policy research and initiatives.”The document continues, “The resulting technology will benefit the public and the corporation will seek to distribute it for the public benefit when applicable. The corporation is not organized for the private gain of any person.”Exhibit No. 70An April 2016 email exchange between Musk and Nvidia CEO Jensen Huang. Musk asks Huang if the OpenAI team can buy an early unit of a supercomputer, making sure to highlight that “OpenAI is unaffiliated with Tesla. It is a non-profit funded by me and a few others with the goal of developing safe AGI (and hopefully not paving the road to hell with good intentions).”Huang responds that he “will make sure OpenAI gets one of the first ones.”Exhibit No. 388A photo of Jensen Huang ostensibly dropping off said computer. Elon Musk stands nearby.On the wall behind him is a lengthy quote sometimes attributed to U.S. Navy Admiral Hyman G. Rickover, which is echoed in a 2013 blog post by Altman. (The Verge couldn’t immediately confirm the whole quote was said by Rickover; in a US Navy post attributed to the admiral, only part of the quote appears: “Man has a large capacity for effort. But it is so much greater than we think it is, that few ever reach this capacity.”)*Exhibit No. 152In an August 2017 email exchange between Musk and Shivon Zilis, Musk’s chief of staff who eventually sat on OpenAI’s board, and with whom Musk would eventually share multiple children. Zilis writes a recap of her meeting with Brockman and Sutskever, laying out seven unanswered questions. She says Brockman and Sutskever are fine with Musk spending less time on the company and having less control, or spending more time and having more control, but not less time and more control. They also hope to raise significantly more than $100 million to start, as they worry the data center they need alone would cost that much. She says Brockman is relatively set on an equal equity split. They also, she writes, worry about Musk’s control over the company. In her notes recapping their concerns, Zilis writes, “Is the requirement for absolute control? They wonder if there is a scenario where there could be some sort of creative overrule position if literally everyone else disagreed on direction.”The biggest point of tension, Zilis writes, seems to be on Musk’s duration of control over the company, despite his 51% stake. “*The* non-negotiable seems to be an ironclad agreement to not have any one person have absolute control of AGI if it’s created. Satisfying this means a situation where, regardless of what happens to the three of them, [Greg, Ilya, and Sam] it’s guaranteed that power over the company is distributed after the 2-3 year initial period … An ironclad 2-3yr minority control agreement, regardless of the fates of Greg / Sam / Ilya.”Musk responds, “This is very annoying. Please encourage them to go start a company. I’ve had enough.”*Exhibit No. 153A September 2017 email to Musk from Jared Birchall, an adviser to Musk and manager of his family office. He attaches a “more user friendly version of the cap table that Ilya and Greg are proposing.”In it, Musk is reflected as having 51.20% of equity, with Altman, Sutskever, and Brockman each having 11.01%, respectively. There’s also reserved equity for employees, and the cap table denotes each initial employee’s name or nickname followed by a proposed amount of equity.
items.author: Hayden Field
items.titleZh: 马斯克诉奥特曼案迄今公布的所有证据
items.descriptionZh:
马斯克诉奥特曼案庭审已于本周在加州联邦法院正式开始，随着陪审团审理的推进，一系列作为证据的展品正在逐份公开。这些邮件、照片和公司文件从OpenAI尚未命名的最早期开始，揭示了这家AI实验室创立前后的诸多内幕。核心要点包括：英伟达CEO黄仁勋向OpenAI赠送了一台当时极为抢手的超级计算机；马斯克在很大程度上起草了OpenAI的使命宣言，并深刻影响了其早期架构；OpenAI CEO萨姆·奥尔特曼似乎希望大量依赖Y Combinator作为早期支持；OpenAI总裁格雷格·布罗克曼和首席科学家伊利亚·苏茨克弗曾对马斯克对公司的控制程度感到担忧；而马斯克本人则反复强调非营利性质以及“广泛造福人类”的使命。

马斯克提起的这起备受关注的诉讼，指控奥特曼、布罗克曼以及OpenAI的投资者微软违反了公司的慈善信托、构成欺诈和不正当得利。但其核心问题只有一个：OpenAI是否背离了其创始使命——确保通用人工智能（AGI，即达到或超越人类智能的AI系统）造福全人类。这是马斯克多年来针对OpenAI及其高管提起的一系列法律行动中的最新一起。马斯克与奥特曼、布罗克曼共同创立了OpenAI，并曾是早期投资者。（马斯克还拥有xAI，这是一家直接与OpenAI竞争的AI实验室，隶属于其母公司SpaceX。）

前OpenAI员工以及与两家公司关系密切的人士都在密切关注这起诉讼，因为陪审团审判的结果可能影响OpenAI未来的商业运作方式以及其对快速发展的AI技术的控制。此外，据报道OpenAI和SpaceX都计划在今年上市，因此它们比以往任何时候都更受公众关注。在证据开示阶段，已经挖出了许多令人瞠目的AI行业高管之间的通信，从奥特曼与苏茨克弗的邮件到布罗克曼的日记条目，甚至Meta CEO马克·扎克伯格与马斯克的短信也被公开。而现在，随着庭审正式开始，更多内幕即将曝光。

以下是截至目前已公开的全部展品清单，以及每份展品最重要的关键信息。其中带星号(*)的为最重要展品。

* **展品第5号**：2015年6月奥特曼与马斯克的邮件往来。奥特曼提出了一项五点计划，涉及一家AI实验室，其使命是“创造第一个通用AI，并将其用于个人赋权——即看起来最安全的分布式未来版本。更一般地说，安全应作为首要要求。”他建议从7到10人开始，利用Y Combinator位于山景城的一栋额外建筑。治理方面，奥特曼提名了五名初始成员：他自己、马斯克、比尔·盖茨、皮埃尔·奥米迪亚和达斯汀·莫斯科维茨。“技术将由基金会拥有，并‘为了世界的美好’而使用，在如何应用的意图不明确时，将由我们五人决定，”奥特曼写道。他还补充说，实验室的研究人员将获得“与他们的成果不关联的显著经济回报……这应该能消除一些冲突”，并建议支付“有竞争力的薪水”并授予Y Combinator的股权。他还说需要找一个人“管理团队”，但此人“可能不应该进入治理委员会”。奥特曼接着问马斯克，除了治理角色外是否还会参与AI实验室，可能每月来一次讨论进展，或者至少在招募方面公开表示支持。他以彼得·蒂尔在Y Combinator的“兼职合伙人”身份作为模式。最后，奥特曼提到了一封“监管信”，似乎暗示AI实验室将写一封信呼吁AI监管。他说很乐意不把马斯克列为公开签署人。马斯克回复：“全部同意。”

* **展品第7号**：2015年10月奥特曼与马斯克的邮件。奥特曼建议马斯克先承诺1亿美元，并询问他能否在未来五年额外捐赠3000万美元。他说比尔·盖茨尚未承诺捐款，但希望“下周能把他锁定”，并补充说他相信马克·扎克伯格可能不会参与，因为扎克伯格有自己的AI实验室FAIR。他还建议他和马斯克先作为安全委员会的头两名成员，并在接下来一年再增加三名成员，称之为“发布任何可能危险东西的‘第二把钥匙’”。马斯克回复：“我们来讨论一下治理问题。这至关重要。我不想资助一个最终走向错误方向的项目。”

* **展品第12号**：2015年11月马斯克与奥特曼的邮件。两人讨论即将成立的AI实验室的计划。马斯克开头提到了与“格雷格[布罗克曼]的一次很棒的谈话”，并表示“到目前为止对每个人都印象深刻”，称这是一个“伟大的团队”。他建议将实验室创建为一个“独立的、纯正的501(c)(3)非营利组织，但目标明确，聚焦于广泛向人类传播强AI的积极前景。”他说公司“仍然会在某个时间点追求收入超过成本，但正净收入将流入现金储备。”在员工薪酬方面，马斯克建议现金工资加特定奖金。他说如果奥特曼同意，员工可以将现金转换为Y Combinator的股票，并补充说如果他们宁愿将部分或全部转换为SpaceX的股票也可以。（“SpaceX这边我基本上可以为所欲为，因为它是私有的，谢天谢地。”）他还提供来自特斯拉的“海量真实世界传感器数据”供AI实验室使用，并提到数据量“比任何其他公司高出几个数量级”。马斯克为AI实验室起的第一个名字是“Freemind”，因为这个名字“传达了这样一种感觉：我们正在努力创造数字智能，并将其免费提供给所有人——这与Deepmind的‘一统天下’方法相反。”他还说会投入任何有用的时间，即使这意味着减少分配给SpaceX和特斯拉的时间。“如果我真的相信这可能是最大的近期存在性威胁，那么行动就应该追随信念。”他后来补充说，尽管似乎试图做一个沉默的合伙人，但他必须“硬着头皮承认真正的参与。这对许多人来说会是一个冲击，但就这样吧。不能对这件事不冷不热。”奥特曼建议AI实验室与Y Combinator共用一栋楼，并利用其法律团队来帮助启动。他还建议名称“Axon”或与著名计算机科学家和数学家艾伦·图灵相关的名字。马斯克写道：“某个与图灵相关但又不太吓人的名字可能不错。不过要避免图灵测试的联想，因为那听起来像是在取代人类。”

* **展品第14号**：2015年12月奥特曼与马斯克之间关于OpenAI使命宣言和新闻稿开头段落的邮件。马斯克说“这份发布的全部目的就是为了吸引顶尖人才”。两人在措辞上来回修改，最终成果与马斯克的原始草稿相差不大。马斯克在草稿中写道：“这一事业的结果是不确定的，与其他人提供的报酬相比薪水很低，但我们相信目标和结构是正确的。”奥特曼在草稿中写道：“因为没有任何财务义务，我们可以专注于最大的人类正面影响，并尽可能广泛地传播AI技术。”

* **展品第16号**：OpenAI于2015年12月8日提交的正式公司章程。文件声明OpenAI“应是一个专为慈善目的而组织的非营利公司”，其目的是“确保通用人工智能造福全人类，包括通过开展和/或资助人工智能研究。公司还可以研究和/或以其他方式支持安全开发及分发此类技术及其相关益处的工作，包括分析该技术的社会影响，并支持相关的教育、经济和安全政策研究及倡议。”文件继续写道：“由此产生的技术将使公众受益，公司将寻求在适用时将其用于公共利益。公司不为任何人的私人利益而组织。”

* **展品第70号**：2016年4月马斯克与英伟达CEO黄仁勋的邮件。马斯克询问OpenAI团队是否可以购买一台超级计算机的早期样机，并特意强调“OpenAI与特斯拉无关。它是一个由我和其他几人资助的非营利组织，目标是开发安全的AGI（并希望不要好心铺就通向地狱的道路）。”黄仁勋回复说：“我将确保OpenAI得到第一批中的一台。”

* **展品第388号**：一张照片显示黄仁勋亲自送来上述计算机，埃隆·马斯克站在附近。他身后的墙上有一段长引语，有时被归功于美国海军上将海曼·G·里科弗，这段引语也出现在奥特曼2013年的一篇博客中。（The Verge无法立即确认整段引语是否由里科弗所说；在美国海军一篇归功于该上将的文章中，只出现了部分引语：“人类有巨大的努力能力。但它比我们想象的要大得多，以至于很少人能达到这种能力。”）

* **展品第152号**：2017年8月马斯克与其幕僚长希冯·齐利斯（齐利斯后来进入OpenAI董事会，并与马斯克育有多个子女）之间的邮件。齐利斯总结了与布罗克曼和苏茨克弗的会面，列出了七个未解决的问题。她说布罗克曼和苏茨克弗对马斯克投入较少时间并拥有较少控制权、或者投入较多时间并拥有较多控制权都无所谓，但不能接受投入较少时间却拥有较多控制权。他们还希望初始融资远超过1亿美元，因为他们担心仅数据中心一项就需要这么多钱。她说布罗克曼相对坚持股权平均分配。他们还担心马斯克对公司的控制。在总结他们担忧的笔记中，齐利斯写道：“是否需要绝对控制？他们想知道，如果其他所有人真的都不同意方向，是否存在某种创造性的否决权场景？”齐利斯写道，最大的紧张点似乎是马斯克对公司控制的时间长度，尽管他拥有51%的股份。“*不可谈判的是*，必须有一份铁定的协议，确保在创造AGI时没有任何一个人拥有绝对控制权。满足这一点意味着一个局面：无论他们三（格雷格、伊利亚和萨姆）发生什么，在最初2-3年之后，公司的权力必须被分散……一份铁定的2-3年少数控制协议，无论格雷格/萨姆/伊利亚的命运如何。”马斯克回复：“这非常烦人。请鼓励他们自己成立公司。我受够了。”

* **展品第153号**：2017年9月，马斯克的顾问兼其家族办公室负责人贾里德·伯查尔发给马斯克一封邮件，附上了一张“伊利亚和格雷格提议的、更用户友好的股权结构表”。表中显示马斯克持有51.20%的股权，奥特曼、苏茨克弗和布罗克曼各持有11.01%。另外还为员工预留了股权，表格中注明了每位初始员工的名字或昵称及其建议的股权数量。

items.title: Meta's multi-billion-dollar Graviton deal highlights intensifying CPU shortages in AI infrastructure — the industry signals a shift to Agentic inference workloads, pushing demand
items.description: Meta signed a multibillion-dollar, multi-year deal with Amazon Web Services last week to deploy tens of millions of Graviton5 CPU cores across AWS data centers.
items.content: Share Copy link Facebook X Whatsapp Reddit Pinterest Flipboard Email Share this article Join the conversation Follow us Add us as a preferred source on Google Newsletter Subscribe to our newsletter Meta signed a multibillion-dollar, multi-year deal with Amazon Web Services last week to deploy tens of millions of Graviton5 CPU cores across AWS data centers, making Meta one of the five largest Graviton customers worldwide. The deal focuses explicitly on CPU-intensive agentic AI workloads, not GPU training, with Amazon CEO Andy Jassy saying in a post accompanying the announcement that agentic AI is “becoming almost as big a CPU story as a GPU story.”Meta already has GPU and accelerator contracts worth hundreds of billions across Nvidia, AMD, Broadcom, Google, CoreWeave, and Nebius, and it went to AWS specifically for general-purpose CPUs. Santosh Janardhan, Meta's head of infrastructure, said in the joint announcement that "diversifying our compute sources is a strategic imperative," and that Graviton allows the company to "run the CPU-intensive workloads behind agentic AI with the performance and efficiency we need at our scale."Graviton5, which AWS unveiled at re: Invent in December, packs 192 Arm Neoverse V3 cores on a 3nm process with roughly 180 MB of L3 cache, a fivefold increase over Graviton4. AWS claims a 25% performance lift over its predecessor and 33% lower inter-core latency. AWS vice president Nafea Bshara confirmed that the contract runs for at least three years and that the majority of capacity will be deployed in the U.S.Article continues below You may like CPU requirements for AI workloads are multiplying, driving intensifying shortages and price hikes Inside Meta and AMD's $100 billion deal, and why AMD is giving up a slice of the company in return for GPU orders ‘CPUs are cool again,' Intel and AMD reporting spikes in CPU demand due to agentic AI The CPU-to-GPU ratioThe meteoric rise of agentic AI is driving notable shifts in CPU-to-GPU ratios. While training LLMs relies on large deployments of GPUs, agentic inference is fundamentally different, involving processes like branching control flow, tool invocation, sandbox execution, validation loops, and orchestration across many concurrent sub-agents. All that work falls on CPUs.In its recent earnings call, Intel’s CFO David Zinsner said that the ratios of CPUs to GPUs in data centers have already moved from 1:8 to 1:4, adding that as workloads continue migrating towards inference and agentic AI, ratios could converge to 1:1 or even tilt further in favor of CPUs. “As you think about the growth rate now going forward, it’s [CPU demand] going to become a significant part of the AI [total addressable market],” Zinsner said.Arm has also quantified the rising demand for agentic AI in terms of core counts. At the company’s Arm Everywhere event in March, Arm launched its first in-house silicon product, the 136-core AGI CPU, with Meta as lead partner and customer. Arm CEO Rene Haas told the audience that a typical AI data center today requires around 30 million CPU cores per gigawatt of capacity. With agentic workloads, however, that figure rises to roughly 120 million cores per gigawatt, a fourfold increase driven by agents that run continuously, spawn sub-agents, and generate queries at more than 15 times the rate of human chatbot users.Meanwhile, AMD CEO Lisa Su said at the Morgan Stanley TMT Conference in March that "we're seeing a significant CPU demand, frankly, as a result of the inference demand picking up." She added that "the CPU portion of the business has actually far exceeded my expectations in terms of demand."Supply constraints and rising lead times (Image credit: Amazon Web Services)The surge in CPU demand is running into a supply chain that planned for a GPU-dominated world, leading to server CPU lead times stretching to roughly six months, up from about two weeks before the agentic demand spike.Intel acknowledged on its Q1 earnings call that unmet Xeon demand "starts with a B," referring to billions of dollars in lost revenue, with CEO Lip-Bu Tan saying that “In recent months, we have seen clear signs that the CPU is reinserting itself as the indispensable foundation of the AI era." Revenue would have been higher had Intel been able to produce more chips, the company said: Q1 data center and AI revenue came in at $5.05 billion, up 22% year-over-year.Server CPU prices have climbed 10% to 20% since March, with analysts expecting a further 8% to 10% increase in the second half of the year. Intel raised prices in both February and March, with a third increase reportedly planned for May, bringing the cumulative hike to roughly 30% above 2025 levels. AMD’s Lisa Su told the Morgan Stanley audience that AMD's own customers described the demand as something that "was perhaps… under-forecasted," adding: "We are in the process of catching up." What to read next Intel, AMD server CPUs reportedly suffering from supply shortages in China PC makers face shortages of Intel and AMD CPUs that stretch up to six months Meta's new MTIA lineup joins hyperscalers' unified push for dedicated inferencing chips The bottleneck extends well beyond CPUs themselves, however, with TrendForce downgrading its full-year server shipment growth forecast from 20% to 13%, per reporting from The Register, because power management ICs and baseboard management controllers needed to assemble complete servers are stretching to 35- to 40-week lead times.Foundries are prioritizing higher-margin AI-specific chips, squeezing capacity for the mature-node components that general-purpose servers require. Samsung's planned closure of its S7 eight-inch wafer fab in Korea will tighten PMIC supply further. Even with all the GPUs and HBM in the world, you can’t ship a rack without the host CPUs, PMICs, and BMCs.Compute diversificationIn response to this, Meta is seemingly attempting to spread its CPU procurement across every available source. In addition to the Graviton5 deal, Meta co-developed the Arm AGI CPU announced in March and plans to deploy it alongside its Broadcom-built MTIA inference accelerators, and the company has struck a $100 billion deal with AMD that includes EPYC server CPUs and Instinct GPUs. Nvidia also announced that Meta will deploy standalone Grace CPUs in production, with Vera to follow. Intel and Google separately announced a multi-year Xeon collaboration in early April, further demonstrating how x86 supply is being locked up through long-term agreements across the industry.Nvidia's decision to launch its 88-core Vera CPU as a standalone product, separate from its GPU systems, reflects the same dynamic, with Jensen Huang saying that he expects Vera to become a multibillion-dollar business at GTC in March. This, in addition to Arm breaking 35 years of pure IP-licensing precedent to ship finished silicon, and Intel redirecting wafer capacity to Xeon, shows that all the major players are either manufacturing or securing long-term supply of CPUs for agentic workloads.In terms of infrastructure spending, CreditSights projects that the top five hyperscalers will spend roughly $750 billion on capex in 2026, up around 67% year-over-year. Amazon alone has guided to $200 billion, and Meta has set a range of $115 to $135 billion. Most of that is naturally destined for AI, with every gigawatt of agentic capacity requiring four times the CPU cores of traditional AI training clusters.Meta's Graviton deal is a sign, by the company spending more aggressively on AI infrastructure than almost anyone else, that its own supply can’t deliver enough general-purpose compute to keep pace.
items.author: Luke James
items.titleZh: Meta数十亿美元的Graviton交易凸显AI基础设施中CPU短缺加剧——行业信号转向代理推理工作负载，推动需求
items.descriptionZh:
Meta与亚马逊网络服务(AWS)上周签署了一项价值数十亿美元、为期多年的合作协议，计划在AWS数据中心内部署数千万颗Graviton5 CPU核心，使Meta成为全球五大Graviton客户之一。这笔交易明确聚焦于CPU密集型的人工智能代理工作负载，而非GPU训练。亚马逊CEO安迪·贾西在相关公告中表示，代理型AI“正变得几乎与GPU一样重要的CPU故事”。

Meta已与英伟达、AMD、博通、谷歌、CoreWeave和Nebius等公司签订了价值数百亿美元的GPU和加速器合同，此次却专门为通用CPU与AWS合作。Meta基础设施负责人桑托什·贾纳尔丹在联合声明中指出，“实现计算源的多元化是我们的战略要务”，而Graviton使其能够“以我们规模所需的性能和效率，运行代理型AI背后的CPU密集型工作负载”。

Graviton5于2024年12月在AWS re:Invent大会上发布，采用3纳米制程，集成192个Arm Neoverse V3核心，三级缓存约180 MB，是上一代Graviton4的五倍。AWS宣称其性能比前代提升25%，核间延迟降低33%。AWS副总裁纳菲亚·布沙拉证实，合同期限至少为三年，大部分产能将在美国部署。

### CPU需求激增与比例变化

代理型AI的迅速崛起正显著改变数据中心中CPU与GPU的比例。训练大语言模型依赖大规模GPU部署，但代理推理完全不同：它涉及分支控制流、工具调用、沙箱执行、验证循环和多并发子代理编排等过程，所有这些工作均落在CPU上。英特尔CFO戴维·津斯纳在最近的财报电话会议上表示，数据中心CPU与GPU的比例已从1:8变为1:4，随着工作负载持续向推理和代理型AI迁移，该比例可能收敛至1:1，甚至进一步偏向CPU。“展望未来增长率，CPU需求将成为AI总体可寻址市场的重要部分。”

Arm公司也用量化数据说明了代理型AI的强劲需求。在3月的Arm Everywhere活动上，Arm推出了其首款自研芯片产品——136核AGI CPU，Meta作为主要合作伙伴和客户。Arm CEO雷内·哈斯透露，目前一个典型的AI数据中心每吉瓦容量约需3000万CPU核心；而代理型工作负载下，这一数字升至约1.2亿核心——增长4倍，原因是代理持续运行、生成子代理，且查询速率是人工聊天机器人用户的15倍以上。AMD CEO苏姿丰在3月的摩根士丹利TMT大会上同样指出，“我们看到CPU需求显著增长，坦率说，这是推理需求上升的结果”，并补充称“CPU业务的实际需求远超我的预期”。

### 供应链紧张与价格上涨

CPU需求的激增正遭遇为GPU主导世界设计的供应链，导致服务器CPU交货期从代理需求激增前的约两周延长至约六个月。英特尔在第一季度财报电话会议上承认，未满足的Xeon需求“以Billion（十亿）为单位”——即数十亿美元的营收损失。CEO陈立武表示：“近几个月来，我们看到了明确信号：CPU正在重新确立其作为AI时代不可或缺的基础地位。”英特尔称若能生产更多芯片，营收本可更高：一季度数据中心和AI营收为50.5亿美元，同比增长22%。

自3月以来，服务器CPU价格已上涨10%至20%，分析师预计下半年还将进一步上涨8%至10%。英特尔在2月和3月连续提价，据称5月计划第三次涨价，累计涨幅或达2025年水平之上的30%。苏姿丰对摩根士丹利听众表示，AMD的客户将需求描述为“或许……被低估了”，并称“我们正在追赶”。

瓶颈远不止CPU本身。据The Register报道，TrendForce已将全年服务器出货量增长预期从20%下调至13%，原因是电源管理IC(PMIC)和基板管理控制器(BMC)等服务器组装所需部件的交货期已延至35至40周。代工厂优先生产利润率更高的AI专用芯片，挤压了通用服务器所需成熟节点组件的产能。三星计划关闭其在韩国的S7八英寸晶圆厂，将进一步收紧PMIC供应。即使拥有所有GPU和HBM，缺少主机CPU、PMIC和BMC也无法出货一台机架。

### 计算多元化布局

面对此局面，Meta正试图让CPU采购覆盖所有可用来源。除Graviton5协议外，Meta还联合开发了3月发布的Arm AGI CPU，计划将其与博通制造的MTIA推理加速器一同部署；与AMD达成价值1000亿美元的协议，涵盖EPYC服务器CPU和Instinct GPU；英伟达也宣布Meta将在生产中独立部署Grace CPU，后续将跟进Vera。英特尔和谷歌在4月初单独宣布了多年Xeon合作，表明x86供应正通过全行业的长期协议被锁定。

英伟达决定将88核Vera CPU作为独立产品推出（不捆绑GPU系统），同样反映了这一趋势。黄仁勋在3月的GTC上预计Vera将成为数十亿美元的业务。Arm打破了35年来纯IP授权的先例，直接交付成品芯片；英特尔则将晶圆产能转向Xeon。所有主要玩家要么在制造CPU，要么在为代理型工作负载锁定长期CPU供应。

资本支出方面，CreditSights预测前五大超大规模厂商2026年将投入约7500亿美元，同比增长约67%。亚马逊一家就规划了2000亿美元，Meta的区间为1150至1350亿美元。这些资金绝大部分流向AI，而每吉瓦代理型能力所需的CPU核心是传统AI训练集群的四倍。Meta的Graviton协议表明，这家在AI基础设施上比几乎所有对手都更激进投入的公司，其自身供应已无法提供足够多的通用计算资源来跟上需求步伐。

items.title: OpenAI has effectively abandoned first-party Stargate data centers in favor of more flexible deals  — company now prefers to lease compute and says Stargate is an umbrella term
items.description: OpenAI has reportedly modified its arrangement on several Stargate projects, leaving the direct ownership set up and instead preferring to lease compute from other partners who took on the direct risk of investing in the infrastructure.
items.content: Copy link Facebook X Whatsapp Reddit Pinterest Flipboard Email Share this article 0 Join the conversation Follow us Add us as a preferred source on Google Newsletter Subscribe to our newsletter In early 2025, OpenAI announced Stargate, a joint venture with Oracle and SoftBank, which aimed to invest $500 billion in AI data centers in the United States. But after more than a year of challenges and disagreements, it seems that the startup has abandoned the original idea of directly owning infrastructure alongside its two partners. According to the Financial Times, OpenAI now prefers to rely on third-party providers and lease capacity in the long term.This is a sensible idea for the startup, which is burning through cash and has reportedly missed internal revenue targets in recent months. But it has also caused chaos among its partners and put its reliability into question. According to the report, OpenAI has "in practice... abandoned the joint venture," choosing instead large bilateral deals with Oracle and more. One person involved with Stargate reportedly said the company had "sidelined first-party data centres," while OpenAI itself admitted that Stargate is merely an "umbrella for our compute strategy."Stargate’s initial goal was to build 20 data centers, with the first project at Abilene, Texas, already operational. However, the three partners reportedly squabbled among themselves for months as they could not agree on who would have ultimate control of the planned data centers. In the end, SoftBank agreed to own and develop the Texas data center, while OpenAI would design and operate it on a long-term lease.Article continues below You may like Stargate AI data centers for OpenAI reportedly delayed by squabbles between partners OpenAI's massive Stargate data center canceled as firm can't reach terms with Oracle, operator struggles with reliability issues OpenAI couldn’t finance its data centers, so it took control of the hardware instead Other Stargate projects located in other areas have also been hit by uncertainties. The UK government signed a deal with OpenAI, among other partners, to build a data center in the UK, but the startup has put it on hold earlier this month. It cited “restrictive regulations” and “high energy costs” as the reason behind the move, but UK AI Minister Kanishka Narayan told the Financial Times that the “only thing that has changed [since] the moment of those commitments…has been the financing environment for OpenAI.”It has also done the same for another Stargate project in Narvik, Norway, with Microsoft stepping up to take over the lease for the site. OpenAI will then lease compute capacity from Redmond, instead of getting it directly from Nscale, the British company that developed the site and also worked on the canceled UK project.All these changes have got some partners “feeling let down and misled by OpenAI,” a person familiar with Microsoft’s decision said. Thankfully, the software giant has stepped in on some of the projects that the startup has supposedly abandoned. One source told the publication that money is not unlimited, no matter what Sam Altman might say, while another said that they prefer Microsoft over OpenAI as a tenant, as “they are more creditworthy.”Even though OpenAI has made a name for itself in AI, the startup has not turned a profit since it was founded in 2015. Many institutions believe in its potential, though, with the firm securing $110 billion in its latest funding round — the biggest amount secured in Silicon Valley history and $10 billion more than what the company initially targeted. Still, some analysts estimate that it could run out of cash by mid-2027 with the massive amounts of money it’s been throwing around to secure more compute.Stay On the Cutting Edge: Get the Tom's Hardware NewsletterGet Tom's Hardware's best news and in-depth reviews, straight to your inbox.Contact me with news and offers from other Future brandsReceive email from us on behalf of our trusted partners or sponsorsAnthropic CEO Dario Amodei criticized moves like this, saying that some of his company’s rivals are pushing infrastructure investments too far. However, OpenAI says that it’s ahead of the exponential compute curve, allowing it to have an advantage over everyone else. For example, Anthropic has had to limit access to some features on its various products due to limited resources, and Amodei has had to spend more on securing capacity to satisfy the increasing demandThe biggest difference between startups, like OpenAI and Anthropic, and their more established rivals, like Microsoft, Google, Meta, and Amazon, is cash flow. The startups still rely on external funding to fuel their growth, while the big tech companies have billion-dollar revenue that they can rely on to pour into expensive hardware and infrastructure projects. Follow Tom's Hardware on Google News, or add us as a preferred source, to get our latest news, analysis, & reviews in your feeds.
items.author: Jowi Morales
items.titleZh: OpenAI实际上已放弃自有的星门数据中心，转而寻求更灵活的合作协议——公司现在更倾向于租用算力，并称“星门”是一个统称
items.descriptionZh:
2025年初，OpenAI宣布与甲骨文（Oracle）和软银（SoftBank）共同成立“星门”（Stargate）合资企业，计划在美国投资5000亿美元建设AI数据中心。然而经过一年多分歧与挑战，这家初创公司似乎已放弃由合资企业直接拥有基础设施的原始构想。据《金融时报》报道，OpenAI更倾向于依赖第三方供应商，并通过长期租赁获取算力容量。

对于现金流持续消耗、近期据称未能实现内部收入目标的OpenAI而言，这一转向合情合理，但也给合作伙伴造成混乱，并引发对其可靠性的质疑。报道称，OpenAI“实际上已放弃合资企业”，转而与甲骨文等伙伴单独签订大规模双边协议。一位参与星门项目的人士透露，公司已将自建数据中心“边缘化”，而OpenAI自身也承认，星门目前仅是“其计算战略的统称”。

星门的初始目标是在全美建设20个数据中心，首个项目位于德克萨斯州阿比林市，目前已投入运营。然而，三方伙伴数月来就规划中数据中心的最终控制权争执不下。最终，软银同意拥有并开发德克萨斯数据中心，而OpenAI负责设计并以长期租赁形式运营。其他地区的星门项目也遭遇不确定性：英国政府曾与OpenAI等合作伙伴签署在英建设数据中心协议，但本月早些时候OpenAI以“严格监管”和“高能源成本”为由暂停该项目。英国人工智能大臣卡尼什卡·纳拉扬则认为，“自签约以来，唯一改变的就是OpenAI的融资环境”。此外，挪威纳尔维克的星门项目也遭同样命运，微软接手该站点租赁权，OpenAI将向微软租赁算力，而非直接向原开发方英国公司Nscale购买。

这些变化导致部分合作伙伴“感到被OpenAI辜负和误导”。一位知情人士称，微软已介入部分被OpenAI放弃的项目，另一位人士直言：“钱不是无限的，无论萨姆·奥特曼怎么说。”相比于OpenAI，合作伙伴更愿意选择微软作为租户，因为其“信用更好”。

尽管OpenAI在AI领域声名鹊起，但自2015年成立以来尚未实现盈利。不过许多机构仍看好其潜力——在最新一轮融资中，OpenAI获得1100亿美元，创下硅谷史上最大融资纪录，超出最初目标100亿美元。但分析师估计，鉴于其为获取算力持续投入巨额资金，OpenAI可能在2027年年中耗尽现金。

Anthropic CEO达里奥·阿莫代伊批评此类举动，认为部分竞争对手的基建投资过度。而OpenAI则称自己处于指数级算力增长的前沿，因而具备竞争优势——例如Anthropic因资源限制不得不限制某些功能的使用，阿莫代伊也需花费更多成本获取算力以满足需求。初创公司（如OpenAI和Anthropic）与成熟巨头（微软、谷歌、Meta、亚马逊）的最大区别在于现金流：前者依赖外部融资推动增长，后者则拥有数十亿美元收入支撑昂贵的硬件与基础设施项目。

items.title: Google Photos launches an AI try-on feature for clothes you already have
items.description:
Google Photos is launching a new AI-powered feature you can use to virtually try on clothes you already have. Using the photos in your gallery, Google will create a virtual "wardrobe," allowing you to mix and match outfits, save the looks you like, and share them with friends.
A video shared by Google shows how Photos organizes your outfits and individual pieces of clothing into a virtual "wardrobe." You can browse through the outfits you were captured wearing, as well as create new ones by choosing from tops, bottoms, skirts, dresses, and shoes to put together a new look.

Read the full story at The Verge.
items.content: Google Photos is launching a new AI-powered feature you can use to virtually try on clothes you already have. Using the photos in your gallery, Google will create a virtual “wardrobe,” allowing you to mix and match outfits, save the looks you like, and share them with friends.A video shared by Google shows how Photos organizes your outfits and individual pieces of clothing into a virtual “wardrobe.” You can browse through the outfits you were captured wearing, as well as create new ones by choosing from tops, bottoms, skirts, dresses, and shoes to put together a new look.You can also select the button in the bottom right corner of each outfit to virtually try them on.Though Google launched an AI virtual try-on feature last year, it only allowed you to try on clothing you’re shopping for in Search. The new “wardrobe” feature in Google Photos is rolling out to Android devices later this summer before expanding to iOS.
items.author: Emma Roth
items.titleZh: Google Photos推出AI试穿功能，可试穿你已有的衣服
items.descriptionZh:
Google Photos 即将推出一项全新 AI 功能，允许用户利用已有照片实现虚拟试穿。该功能基于你相册中的图像，自动构建一个虚拟“衣橱”，将你在不同照片中穿过的上衣、下装、裙装、鞋履等单品分类整理，并支持自由组合搭配、保存心仪造型以及与好友分享。

从 Google 官方演示视频来看，系统能智能识别并提取照片中的每一件衣物，将其归入“衣橱”中的不同类别。用户不仅可以浏览自己曾穿过的完整搭配，还能通过选择上衣、下装、裙子和鞋子等单品，自由拼装出全新造型。每套搭配的右下角设有“虚拟试穿”按钮，点击后即可预览上身效果。

这一功能的本质是计算机视觉与生成式 AI 的结合。与去年 Google 推出的搜索虚拟试穿功能不同（后者仅限在 Search 中试穿在售商品），新功能直接利用用户自有照片，无需额外拍摄或上传商品图。其核心技术原理可能包括：**物体分割与识別模型**（如 Mask R-CNN 或类似架构）从照片中精准抠出衣物轮廓；**图像生成模型**（如扩散模型或 GAN）将不同衣物自然融合到用户身体上，并处理遮挡、光照、纹理等细节；同时还需要**姿态估计与形变模型**，使衣物贴合用户在不同照片中的身体姿势与角度。这些环节对 AI 芯片的推理能力（特别是 GPU 或 NPU 上的并行计算）要求较高，尤其在处理高分辨率照片以及实时生成多种搭配时，需要低延迟、高吞吐的算力支持。

性能与对比方面，虽然未公布具体指标（如生成速度、准确率），但相比去年只能试穿在售商品的版本，新功能在数据来源上更个性化、场景更多样，对模型泛化能力提出了更高挑战。Google 很可能使用了自家 Tensor 芯片（Pixel 设备）或云端 TPU 进行推理，以保障用户体验。

技术影响与应用场景广泛：首先，它极大降低了用户“整理衣橱、搭配穿搭”的门槛，尤其适合喜欢日常记录穿搭的消费者，可替代手动截图拼图或第三方穿搭 App；其次，对于时尚电商而言，这一功能若开放接口，有望成为个性化推荐的新入口——系统能根据用户实际拥有的衣物推荐搭配方案或搭配补全建议；此外，在社交场景中，用户可以更直观地向朋友展示自己的穿搭创意，甚至促成二手闲置衣物的交换。据悉，该功能将于今年夏季末率先在 Android 设备上推出，随后扩展至 iOS 平台。

items.title: Live updates from Elon Musk and Sam Altman’s court battle over the future of OpenAI
items.description:
Sam Altman and Elon Musk are facing off in a high-stakes trial that could alter the future of OpenAI and its most well-known product, ChatGPT. In 2024, Musk filed a lawsuit accusing OpenAI of abandoning its founding mission of developing AI to benefit humanity and shifting focus to boosting profits instead. 
The trial began with jury selection on April 27th, before Elon Musk took the stand on Tuesday as the first witness called, portraying his interest in founding OpenAI as an effort to help save humanity, before returning to the stand on Wednesday.
Musk was a cofounder of OpenAI and claims that Altman and cofounder Greg Brockman tricked him into giving the company money, only to turn their backs on their original goal. However, OpenAI says that “This lawsuit has always been a baseless and jealous bid to derail a competitor” in a bid to boost Musk’s own SpaceX / xAI / X companies that have launched Grok as a competitor to ChatGPT.
In his lawsuit, Musk is asking for the removal of Altman and Brockman, and for OpenAI to stop operating as a public benefit corporation. Musk has also demanded that OpenAI’s nonprofit receive up to $150 billion in damages he’s asking for if he wins the case.
Here’s all the latest on the trial between Musk and Altman:
Don’t worry about Tesla’s robot army!
			
“You mostly do unfair questions.”
			
“It’s a free country.”
			
“Will you answer my question?”
			
Musk’s desire for control comes up again.
			
“This is a hypothetical.”
			
All the evidence unveiled so far in Musk v. Altman
			
Did Musk initially envision OpenAI as a corporation?
			
Musk is being combative on cross already
			
“I did say that I would commit up to a billion dollars, yes.”
			
Is Tesla really not working on AGI?
			
Musk is returning to the stand.
			
At times, being a judge is much like being a kindergarden teacher.
			
We’re on a break.
			
“I mean, all due respect to Microsoft, do you really want Microsoft controlling digital superintelligence?”
			
“What’s going on here this is a bait and switch.”
			
A Musk-Altman spat about Microsoft.
			
Musk really cannot help himself.
			
“Capped profit” wasn’t an issue, even when Microsoft got involved.
			
“Tesla is not pursuing AGI.”
			
Musk is more on his game today.
			
“After I received these reassurances that OpenAI would continue to be a non-profit I continued to donate over $10 million.”
			
“I actually was a fool who provided free funding for them to create a startup.”
			
More discussion of who would own OpenAI.
			
“I don’t lose my temper,” says Elon Musk.
			
“2017 was a hard year, and we’ve made mistakes.”
			
“I formed many for-profit tech companies, and could have done so with OAI.”
			
“Crystal clear focus.”
			
Sam Altman has just entered the room, right ahead of the jury.
			
A member of the public just got dressed down by YGR about taking photos.
			
Musk v Altman et al. is back in session.
			
In naming OpenAI, Elon Musk worried anything related to the Turing Test could mean bad PR.
			
Elon Musk appeared more petty than prepared
			
That’s a wrap!
			
YGR scolds OpenAI for taking inconsistent positions on the origin of its name.
			
Elon Musk tells the jury that all he wants to do is save humanity
			
Arguments over ownership.
			
Apparently OpenAI could have had an ICO.
			
“I was not averse to a small for-profit,” Musk says.
			
We’re reading emails between Musk and Jensen Huang.
			
Musk says nonprofit was non-negotiable for OpenAI.
			
We’re at the founding of OpenAI.
			
Musk says he would have created something like OpenAI on his own.
			
Musk recalls meeting Sam Altman.
			
Sam Altman left during a break, but Elon Musk’s lawyer didn’t notice.
			
“Here we are in 2026 and AI is scary smart.”
			
“I have extreme concerns about AI,” says Musk.
			
AI will be as smart as ‘any human as soon as next year.’
			
Musk claims he has time for SpaceX, Tesla, Neuralink, and the Boring Company because he works a lot.
			
Musk is telling the jury he (co)founded Tesla.
			
Neuralink’s long-term goal is… AI?
			
“There need to be things that people are excited about that make life worth living … being out there among the stars can excite everyone.”
			
A little Musk biography.
			
Elon Musk, looking funereal in a black suit with a black tie, says “it’s not okay to steal a charity.”
			
Elon Musk takes the stand in high-profile trial against OpenAI
			
We are back from a break.
			
Elon Musk will be the first witness in Musk v. Altman.
			
“Microsoft unlocked with OpenAI a virtuous cycle.”
			
Microsoft enters the chat.
			
“We are here because Mr Musk didn’t get his way at OpenAI.”
			
“[Musk] demanded control, he demanded the ability to make all the decisions without regard to the other founders.”
			
OpenAI lawyers argue that Elon was right in the middle of discussions about a for-profit pivot.
			
“Musk was furious that OpenAI succeeded.”
			
OpenAI: Musk’s lawsuit is a “pageant of hypocrisy.”
			
Sam Altman’s “related party conflicted transactions” are how he made money on OpenAI, Molo says.
			
Technical difficulties.
			
OpenAI is like a museum store that has looted the Picassos and pocketed the profits.
			
AGI might be out of fashion in the AI world, but it will be at the center of this trial
			
“The defendants in this case stole a charity.”
			
Musk and Altman go to court
			
Good morning from the Musk v Altman line outside the courtroom.
			
Jury selection in Musk v. Altman: ‘People don’t like him’
			
We have a jury.
			
Elon Musk’s lawyer tried to get some jurors thrown out for disliking Musk.
			
Apparently things are exciting outside.
			
We have gone through the first 20 potential jurors.
			
Voir dire has begun.
			
The Elon Musk vs. OpenAI trial starts today.
			
Elon Musk drops fraud claims against OpenAI and Sam Altman before trial.
			
Musk vs. Altman is here, and it’s going to get messy
			
Elon Musk is about to be a very busy boy!
			
‘Sideshow’ concerns and billionaire dreams: What I learned from Elon Musk’s lawsuit against OpenAI
			
Elon Musk’s xAI is suing OpenAI and Apple
			
Inside Elon Musk’s messy breakup with OpenAI

items.author: Elizabeth Lopatto
items.titleZh: 埃隆·马斯克与山姆·奥尔特曼就OpenAI未来展开法庭大战的实时更新
items.descriptionZh:
一场备受瞩目的法律对决正在上演：OpenAI联合创始人埃隆·马斯克与现任CEO萨姆·奥特曼对簿公堂，这场诉讼的最终结果可能深刻改变全球最知名AI产品ChatGPT及其所属公司的未来走向。2024年，马斯克提起诉讼，指控OpenAI背离了其创建时“开发人工智能以造福人类”的初心，转而将重心放在追逐利润上。案件于4月27日启动陪审团遴选，马斯克作为首位出庭证人，在周二和周三连续两天作证，将自己参与创立OpenAI描述为一项“拯救人类”的努力。

马斯克曾是OpenAI的联合创始人，他声称奥特曼与另一位联合创始人格雷格·布罗克曼诱骗他提供资金，随后却背弃了最初的目标。而OpenAI方面则反驳称，“这起诉讼始终是一场毫无根据且出于嫉妒的企图，旨在拖垮竞争对手”，目的是为马斯克自己的企业——SpaceX、xAI以及X公司——提升竞争力，这些公司推出的聊天机器人Grok正是ChatGPT的直接对手。

在诉讼中，马斯克要求法院罢免奥特曼和布罗克曼的职务，并命令OpenAI停止作为公益公司运营。他还提出，若胜诉，OpenAI的非营利实体应获得高达1500亿美元的损害赔偿。这起案件的核心争议点在于：OpenAI从非营利研究机构转型为“有限盈利”商业模式的过程中，是否违背了其创始人最初承诺的“安全、开放、造福全人类”的使命？马斯克认为，与微软的深度合作以及ChatGPT的商业化已经彻底改变了公司的性质，而奥特曼则辩称，为了获取训练先进AI所需的海量计算资源，适度盈利是必要的。

虽然案件本身不涉及具体AI技术原理，但背后映射出人工智能行业一个根本性的张力：如何平衡技术开放与商业利益？ChatGPT作为生成式AI的标杆产品，其影响力已渗透到教育、医疗、金融、客服等各个领域，而马斯克自己的Grok则试图以“更少政治正确束缚”为卖点争夺市场。这场诉讼的判决不仅将决定OpenAI的治理结构，还可能为整个AI行业设定关于“公益使命 vs 商业回报”的法律先例。目前庭审仍在进行中，双方围绕证据、动机和合同条款展开激烈交锋。

items.title: ChatGPT downloads are slowing — and may cause problems for OpenAI’s IPO
items.description:
ChatGPT is struggling to keep up its once-explosive growth as users uninstall the app or opt for rival chatbots instead.
